policy_statement: Review practices around back-up power generation and ensure critical facilities are covered.
mechanism_description: Reviewing back-up power generation practices and ensuring critical facilities are covered helps maintain essential services during power outages and climate-related disruptions.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (e.g., extreme weather) → grid outage/disruption → backup power + continuity planning → ↓ service interruption for critical facilities → ↓ exposure/vulnerability
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the preparedness and operational capacity of critical facilities (e.g., water/wastewater, emergency services, shelters, communications) by assessing needs and installing/maintaining backup power and continuity plans. (2) During extreme weather and other climate-related disruptions that cause grid failures, onsite generation/storage and operational procedures maintain electricity supply to essential loads. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is reduced disruption of essential services and reduced health/safety and economic impacts from climate-driven outages (greater resilience).
dominant_pathway_test: Ensuring backup power for critical facilities most strongly reduces outage-driven service disruption during climate-related hazards, lowering exposure and vulnerability to those hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazards can trigger power outages; backup power resilience planning keeps critical services operating, reducing vulnerability and impact—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why Adaptation over alternatives: while backup systems may have energy/GHG implications (diesel generators vs. batteries), the principal intended and largest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is maintaining service continuity during disruptions, not emissions reduction or resource efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: if implemented via low-carbon microgrids/storage, there can be mitigation co-benefits; if diesel gensets are added, emissions may increase—these do not change the dominant resilience function of the mechanism.  
4) Primary applies to ALL instances: any policy that reviews/provides backup power coverage for critical facilities is fundamentally about continuity under disruptions, which is an adaptation/resilience objective regardless of technology choice.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., energy-system resilience, critical services resilience, health resilience) is deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation specifics.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Backup power can be fossil-fueled (potentially increasing GHG/air pollution) or renewable/storage-based (possible mitigation co-benefits). Classification remains Adaptation because the dominant effect is service continuity under climate-related outages; technology choice affects secondary impacts.
key_indicators: "Review practices"; "back-up power generation"; "ensure critical facilities are covered"
co_benefits: Improved general emergency preparedness and reliability for non-climate-related outages; potential public safety and economic continuity benefits.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Ensure … are covered” could imply a mandate/requirement or capital procurement, but no explicit compliance mechanism, standard, or investment is stated; classified as planning/operational review accordingly.
